attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with her daughter reporting worsening control of her Parkinson disease symptoms over three months. She experiences increasing periods where her medications seem ineffective, with severe tremor and difficulty walking, alternating with periods of good symptom control. These 'off' periods are becoming more frequent and prolonged, particularly before her next medication dose is due.

She has a history of Parkinson disease diagnosed eight years ago with associated cognitive impairment, hypertension, dyslipidaemia and type 2 diabetes. Her medications include levodopa-carbidopa 100/25mg four times daily, metformin, rosuvastatin, amlodipine, and paracetamol.

On examination, she appears well. Her vital signs are within normal limits. She displays a resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, and bradykinesia, isolated to the right side, worse in the upper limb. She is oriented to time, place, and person with intact comprehension. Her gait is slow and shuffling with reduced arm swing.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Switch to levodopa-carbidopa controlled release preparation
Reduce levodopa-carbidopa dose and give six times daily
Increase levodopa-carbidopa dose and give four times daily
Refer for percutaneous pump for levodopa-carbidopa intestinal gel
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with motor fluctuations ('wearing-off' phenomenon) in advanced Parkinson disease, where the duration of benefit from each levodopa dose becomes shorter. The presence of cognitive impairment does not preclude optimisation of motor symptoms, particularly as the patient remains oriented with intact comprehension.

Reduce levodopa-carbidopa dose and give six times daily is the most appropriate first step. This approach maintains the same total daily dose but provides more frequent, smaller doses to achieve more consistent plasma levels and better symptom control. This strategy often helps manage motor fluctuations before considering dose increases or additional medications.

Increase levodopa-carbidopa dose might worsen peak-dose dyskinesias and would not address the fundamental problem of shortened duration of action. Higher doses may also increase the risk of confusion and hallucinations in a patient with cognitive impairment.

Switch to levodopa-carbidopa controlled release preparation often provides less predictable absorption and may worsen motor fluctuations. These preparations are generally less effective than adjusting the timing of standard formulations.

Refer for percutaneous pump for levodopa-carbidopa intestinal gel is an invasive option typically reserved for advanced disease when oral medication adjustments have failed. It would be premature to consider this before optimising oral therapy.

Add amantadine is primarily used to treat levodopa-induced dyskinesias rather than wearing-off phenomena. It can also worsen cognitive function and cause confusion in elderly patients.

The management of motor fluctuations in Parkinson disease requires careful balance between achieving adequate symptom control and avoiding complications, particularly in elderly patients with cognitive impairment. Starting with adjustment of medication timing before making more complex changes is often the safest and most effective approach.
learningPoints: Motor fluctuations ('on-off' phenomena) in Parkinson disease often require adjustment of levodopa timing and dosing rather than dose increases.
Cognitive impairment in Parkinson disease should not preclude optimisation of motor symptoms if orientation and comprehension remain intact.
Adding dopamine agonists in elderly patients requires careful consideration due to increased risk of confusion and hallucinations.
references: Waller S, Williams L, Morales-Briceno H, Fung VS. The initial diagnosis and management of Parkinson's disease. Australian journal of general practice. 2021 Nov 1;50(11):793-890. Available online at ; https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2021/november/diagnosis-and-management-of-parkinsons

Lees A, Tolosa E, Stocchi F, et al. Optimizing levodopa therapy, when and how? Perspectives on the importance of delivery and the potential for an early combination approach. Expert Rev Neurother. 2023;23(1):15-24. doi:10.1080/14737175.2023.2176220. Available online at ; https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36729395/
